article_title: Free open-source apps do everything I need from Microsoft 365, except cost money
core_thesis: The author argues that after subscribing to Microsoft 365 for a year, they found the subscription unnecessary because their workflow only required basic spreadsheet and word processing tasks, which are fully covered by free open-source alternatives like LibreOffice and Proton Drive. The article emphasizes that for many users, the paid features (Copilot, cloud storage, advanced Excel functions) are not worth the cost, and open-source tools provide sufficient functionality without the financial commitment.
detailed_summary: The author, Megan Ellis, describes her experience subscribing to Microsoft 365 in 2025 after years of avoiding it, primarily to access a newer version of Excel. However, she found that she only used Excel and Word occasionally—mainly for tax filing and document viewing—and never utilized the other apps (PowerPoint, Outlook, etc.) or the 1TB cloud storage. Copilot, a key selling point, proved unhelpful for her tax data sorting. After her subscription ended, she switched to LibreOffice (Calc for spreadsheets, Writer for documents) and found it met all her needs, with the only drawback being inconsistent dark mode support. For cloud-accessible documents, she uses Proton Drive, appreciating its privacy focus and open-source nature, though Proton Sheets lacks dark mode. The author concludes that her Microsoft 365 subscription was underutilized and that open-source tools have seamlessly replaced it, saving her money without sacrificing essential functionality.

entities.name: Megan Ellis
entities.role: Author of the article, a software writer at XDA Developers

entities.name: Microsoft 365
entities.role: Paid subscription service for Microsoft Office apps and cloud storage

entities.name: LibreOffice
entities.role: Free open-source office suite used as a replacement for Microsoft 365

entities.name: Proton Drive
entities.role: Privacy-focused cloud storage service with open-source apps, used for online document access

entities.name: Copilot
entities.role: AI assistant integrated into Microsoft 365, found unhelpful by the author

key_data_points: The author subscribed to Microsoft 365 primarily for a newer version of Excel but found that the advanced features (Python in Excel, dark mode) were not essential for her basic spreadsheet tasks like tax filing and expense tracking. (🟢 Relevant to your productivity: if you only need basic office functions, a paid subscription may be overkill.)

Copilot, the AI tool in Microsoft 365, failed to streamline tax filing and was ultimately replaced by Claude, an external AI. (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: AI tools can be unreliable; always have fallback methods.)

LibreOffice Calc provides essential spreadsheet features like data filtering and formulas, which the author uses for sorting transactions and tax calculations. (🟢 Relevant to your programming: LibreOffice supports macros and scripting, which can be leveraged for automation.)

LibreOffice Writer handles word processing and form filling without issues, though dark mode is inconsistent—sometimes text remains dark on a black background. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: if you self-host office tools, LibreOffice is a robust, free option.)

Proton Drive offers cloud storage and online document editing with a focus on privacy, but its Sheets app lacks dark mode, preventing the author from using it as a primary tool. (🟢 Relevant to your cybersecurity: Proton's open-source and privacy-focused approach aligns with security-conscious setups.)

The author did not use the 1TB cloud storage included with Microsoft 365 because she backs up files on her NAS. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: self-hosted NAS solutions can replace cloud storage, reducing subscription costs.)
methodology_steps: Step 1: Evaluate your actual usage of Microsoft 365 apps. The author realized she only used Excel and Word occasionally, making the subscription unnecessary.
Step 2: Choose an open-source alternative. The author selected LibreOffice for its compatibility with Microsoft file formats and ease of use.
Step 3: Install LibreOffice (free from libreoffice.org) and replace Excel with Calc, Word with Writer. For presentations, use Impress; for graphics, use Draw; for databases, use Base.

Step 4: For cloud access, sign up for Proton Drive (free plan offers limited storage) and upload documents you need online. Use Proton's web apps for editing, but be aware of limitations like missing dark mode.
Step 5: Migrate your files to the new tools. The author found that LibreOffice handled her existing .xlsx and .docx files without issues.
Step 6: Adjust to minor differences, such as LibreOffice's inconsistent dark theme. The author accepted this trade-off for the cost savings.
conclusions: The author's experience shows that for users with basic office needs (spreadsheets, word processing), free open-source tools like LibreOffice can fully replace Microsoft 365 without loss of functionality. The key is to assess your actual usage before committing to a subscription.
Microsoft 365's added features (Copilot, cloud storage, advanced Excel) are not valuable if they go unused. The author's underutilization of these features made the subscription a waste of money.

Open-source alternatives offer cost savings and privacy benefits, but may have minor drawbacks (e.g., dark mode inconsistencies). These trade-offs are acceptable for many users, especially those who prioritize budget and data control.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to you as a student and programmer who values cost-effective, open-source solutions. It demonstrates that LibreOffice can handle essential office tasks without a subscription, freeing up funds for other tools or homelab equipment. For your Red Teaming and cybersecurity interests, the article highlights the importance of evaluating tool utility—just as the author found Copilot unhelpful, you should test offensive security tools before relying on them. Additionally, the mention of Proton Drive aligns with privacy-conscious setups, which is valuable for secure communications. The article also reinforces the idea that self-hosted solutions (like NAS) can replace cloud subscriptions, a principle applicable to your homelab.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a self-hosted office suite like Collabora Online or NextCloud with LibreOffice integration to gain cloud access and full control over your documents. This would combine the benefits of open-source software with the convenience of online access, while avoiding the dark mode issues of Proton Sheets. For your Red Teaming practice, you could script LibreOffice macros to automate data extraction or document manipulation during engagements.
